title: The Broken Trophy and the Red Herring

root.narration: The school soccer trophy fell off its shelf and cracked! You are investigating. Clue 1: There is a soccer ball on the floor near the case. Clue 2: The glass door of the case was already unlocked. Clue 3: There are scuff marks on the wall ABOVE the trophy case, like someone climbed up. Clue 4: A janitor says he saw a student bouncing a ball in the hallway earlier. The soccer ball near the case seems like an obvious clue, but the scuff marks on the wall tell a different story. What do you focus on?

choices.text: Focus on the scuff marks above the case. Someone climbed up there, and that is probably what knocked the trophy down. The soccer ball might be unrelated.
Focus on the soccer ball. Someone must have kicked it and hit the trophy case.
Find the kid who was bouncing a ball and blame them immediately.

outcome.headline: You Spotted the Red Herring!
outcome.explanation: The soccer ball was a red herring -- a clue that LOOKS important but actually leads you in the wrong direction! The ball was left there by a different student and had nothing to do with the trophy. The real clue was the scuff marks. A student had climbed on the case to reach something on the high shelf and accidentally knocked the trophy over. Great job not getting distracted by the obvious clue!
outcome.whatToRemember: A red herring is a clue that looks important but actually leads you the wrong way. Always consider whether a clue is really connected to the mystery.

keyTerms.definition: A clue or detail that seems important but actually has nothing to do with the mystery. It distracts you from the real answer.
Something that points you in the wrong direction, even though it seems like it should help.

outcome.headline: You Got Tricked by the Red Herring!
outcome.explanation: The soccer ball seemed like the obvious answer, but it was actually a red herring! The ball was left there by someone else and had nothing to do with the broken trophy. The scuff marks on the wall were the real clue -- someone climbed up and knocked the trophy over. In detective work, the most obvious clue is not always the right one.
outcome.whatToRemember: Be careful with obvious clues. Ask yourself: is this clue really connected to the mystery, or could it be there for a totally different reason?

outcome.headline: Jumping to Conclusions! No Evidence!
outcome.explanation: The kid bouncing a ball might have had nothing to do with it! You followed the most obvious trail without checking all the clues. The soccer ball was a red herring, and the scuff marks on the wall told the true story. A good investigator checks ALL the evidence before pointing fingers.
outcome.whatToRemember: Never rush to blame someone based on the first clue you see. Look at ALL the evidence and watch out for red herrings.
